sentences.text: My name is Mark Grinims.
I'm from the town called Ostrog, Poland.
My name of father is Zev.
My mother's name was Eska.
I had one brother and one sister.
My sister was murdered in 1942 in the town Rovno.
And my brother died in the United States of natural causes.
My sister's name was Sonia, and my brother's name was Henry.
Well, I-- Ostrog in gen-- in general was a very beautiful city, full of meadows, full of lilac trees and apple trees, a lot of grass and rivers.
And it was an idyllic place with no pollution, no noise.
And the people used to come from all over for vacation to Ostrog.
I used to live in a fairly comfortable house, but a big house.
And that's about all I can remember.
My childhood is uneventful per se because I lived very comfortable.
And I attended private public school.
And I attend private high school.
I had friends from different religion, Jewish and non-Jewish.
And I got along very well within the community.

I also belonged to the Betar organization, which was a Zionist organization, when I was a teenager up till the age of-- up to the year 1939 until the Soviets came into our town, where all the temples and all the organizations were disband, especially the Jewish ones.
And that's about all I can remember of my childhood.
My, my sister lived at home.
She went to private high school.
My brother was studying abroad in Austria, medicine, and then in Sorbonne in Paris.
To a point, yes-- not orthodox religious, but religious to a point.
I want a temple only on, on major holidays.
And but I was aware because being part of the Zionist organization, which was very nationalistic, I was involved with all of this.
My father was a pharmacist and until he died in 1935--
--of natural causes.
My mother died when I was fairly young.
And my father remarried about 10 years after my mother died.
And I had a very good stepmother.
So it was very comfortable.
Fanny.
And she came from the south border of Poland, Austria.
So she spoke fluently German.
And that later on would, would have had an influence on me to remain in Ostrog because she didn't believe that the Germans will do what they did.
That's why I remained for a while.
And then I left.
She was killed in the second, the massacre in Ostrog in 1941.
In '22, actually.
Yeah.
But it's only one day difference.
Somehow it mixed up in one day.
And the, the Ostrog-- the Jewish history of Ostrog is very interesting.
It became in the 1400s, circa 1400s in 40, 40, [? 50. ?] And it was quite a few Jews immigrated from Germany, from England.
And they were quite well-off until they were told to leave about 40, 50 years later because there were some conflicts between the Polish king and Lithuanian king.
And they were-- and then they came back 50 years later.
And they were dealing with exporting wheat and tem-- timber and wax and all that to Poland, to, to other countries.
And then in the 1600s or so, we had the massacre of about 7,000 Jews through a Ukrainian Cossack man named Khmelnytsky.
And after the massacre of 7,000 people, about five families remained.
And it took quite a few years-- about 50 or so years-- to establish a new community, Jewish community, in Ostrog.
After that, we still had some little, what you call pogroms.
But the Tatars, which were lived in our area, helped the Jews at that time.
And then in the 16-- in the 1700s and 1800s, Ostrog had about 10,000 or more Jews.
And the, the Jewish community prospered very well.
And it was the center of the Hasidic community and all the schools and all of Poland.
And the more prominent rabbis from all of Poland were-- came to lecture and to teach at the yeshivas in Ostrog.
Then we also had a lot of Zionist organizations, about six or seven of them.
Everybody had different uniforms.
They felt very free.
And this was the golden age-- so to say, up to '39-- of the Jews in Ostrog because they paraded in their uniforms.
They felt very free to express their nationalistic feelings.
So everything was fine until the Soviets came in and disband all of this, and then the war.
And after the Soviets came, naturally, everything changed because you couldn't have any businesses, even though my stepmother was still had the-- she was still alive.
And she still continued to work in that drugstore that my father had.
But then gradually, they closed all those stores because they considered them being capitalistic.

And then it was very hard financially because we only had what we could either sell, you know, [INAUDIBLE], so to say, or exchange different cosmetics or medication products for eggs or butter or bread.
So I didn't feel it as much as others because we had the surplus of a lot of things left over from the drugstore that we can exchange for food products.
But other people had much, much worse.
And then in thirty-- in-- from '39 to '41.
And then in '41, the war broke out with Germany.
And that's how the whole Holocaust started.
Yes.
I had a lot of non-Jewish friends from the public school, high school.
I personally did not feel any.
Some did, maybe.
But it wasn't-- I personally did not feel it, any anti-semitism because I was in the public school, and I had a lot of non-Jewish friends.
So I was quite comfortable in the whole environment, so to say.
Well, the problem started in thirty-- the anti-semitism?
Or in general with the problem with--
Well, when the Germans came in, naturally, it started.
And thousands of Jews from Ostrog fled to Russia with the Red Army.
And I wanted to go, but I was a little bit late.
By the time I arrived in a town about 30, 40 miles from Ostrog, the parachutes landed, and I had to go back to my hometown.
Well, the first one-- when I came back from wherever I, I traveled, the first action started, I think, was August 4, 1941.
And they took all the people out from the homes.
They chased them out, so to say-- the police and the Germans.
They used to have a special what they call [GERMAN] Kommando, which is a subgroup of the SS.
And they took them to a big place near the woods.
And at that time, they concentrated on old people, religious people, anybody who had a beard, or anybody who looked-- the sick people.
They killed them-- it was about 3,000 of them-- in the ravines.
They simply shot them.
A month later-- now, we went back and we start-- people started to work.
So I had to work with some people that I knew from my neighborhoods and rag business.
So we had to collect rags and give them to, to the German army.
So they manufactured something from that.
And then I also worked on trucks collecting furniture from the homes that they acquired-- the Germans-- Jewish homes.
Well, for a while, I did-- the first month, everything was very strange to me because I didn't know what will happen later on.
I thought it's all a temporary type of a thing until everything will get organized in some way.
But after the second time, then I realized what's going on.
Now, the second Aktion was about a month later.
Again, they chased out the people from their homes, and they took them to a place near the-- near the woods.
And this time they concentrated mostly on teenagers, people from schools, young people, most of my Jewish friends.
And they say that they-- this time, they didn't shoot him, but they gassed them in trucks.
Now, at that time I was at that-- at that place too together with all the other people.
But they ask-- the Germans ask if, if you are carpenter, or electrician, or you have any kind of a trade.
And I said, I'm a carpenter.
Because they probably needed carpenters or electricians, so they let me go with a few other people.
No, I was not.
But I knew that they'll need some kind of tradespeople.
So they'll-- so I told them I'm a carpenter.
A friend of mine says he's electrician.
And that's how they let us go back.
After that, I realized that this will be probably the beginning of the end.
And then I felt like I'm in a time warp, so to say.
I didn't know where I am.
Everybody was walking, going to school, walking the streets, living a normal life.
And we are completely isolated.
So it's like unbelievable feeling.
We are-- every day we are adrenaline high, so to say.
We are pushing [? nut ?] from minute to minute trying to survive and doing something.
And that's what happened after the second part.
So you were home?
I mean, you didn't leave?
No, I-- we still-- I had-- I still had my home.
I mean, this was only a month later, so they didn't liquidate anything.

At that time I could-- I tried to sell some of the furniture, piano, this and that or some of the materials we had from home from the drugstore left over to exchange it for gold coins, like [INAUDIBLE] into Mexican pesos, things like that.
Because gold was the more or less exchange for any goods.
So I tried to accumulate as much as I could.
And that's what happened after the second part.
Now, since I knew that this is the beginning of the end, it looked like to me like it's twilight zone.
I didn't know where the heck I am.
So one of my teachers in high school used to be a Ukrainian professor of Latin.
And he became a mayor of the city of Ostrog.
So I tried-- through him and some other people-- they took out some documents from high school, Polish documents, of some sons of the offic-- Polish officers who fled to Poland when the Soviets came.
And they were more or less my age.
So on the basis of their birth certificate and school things, they made for me false papers.
The the name that I assumed is actually an actual name of a-- of a-- of a man who was my age, who going to school.
But his parents were in the army, they left when the Soviets came.
Did you know him?
No.
I didn't know him.
No.
And then they-- Dorothy too was based-- her documents are based on papers which are factual papers of birth certificates and high school records and so forth.
So I had to pay for it, naturally, with gold coins.
But nobody-- first of all, people had an idea that nothing will happen, that maybe this is just a passing thing.
Everything will get organized.
They wouldn't kill everybody.
They already killed everybody, so they'll let those people go.
And I figured, just in case, let me make some papers.
So I was the only one, I think-- I think the only one in Ostrog who did it.
And I also tried through some friends to establish some points of escape in case of something.
Now I had a-- my friend that we went to high school together, but he was in the lower grade.
And I was teaching him German and, and Latin, and I was very close to his family.
And the other friend that we formed that group in high school, the jazz singing group, they helped me a lot later on after I escaped.
Now, after the second Aktion, my sister was in Rovno, which was about 40 miles from my town of Ostrog.
Her husband was killed by the Soviets because he was a known Zionist leader of the Betar.
So she, from Lviv, she-- because he studied law.
And she came to Rovno where my relatives on my mother's side lived.
And I tried to go to her to bring her back to my hometown to Ostrog.
But when I came, this was late-- I remember it was late November because it was raining and snowing.
And I had holes in my boots.
And I came to Rovno, and I was a little bit too late.
They already surrounded the area where the Jews lived.
And I had to escape and walk the streets, did-- not knowing where am I going.
Because if they would find me there-- even though I had some paperss-- I had no place to sleep over.
It was raining, snowing.
And I was cold.
And I was hungry.
Luckily that I met my friend from that singing group who was in charge of a theater in Rovno.
And he was surprised to see me, but he took me right in, put me in the basement.
Got in touch with some other people from my hometown.
They sent this horse and wagon.
And they took me up from the basement during the night and brought me back to Ostrog.
That's the fellow that I visited in Warsaw several years ago.
Now he became Vice Minister of Culture in Poland after the war.
And he was very happy to see me and all that.
The other friend of mine who helped me tremendously, the, the one that I was tutoring, and was still from high school, he had-- he had cousins in a city, again, 20, 30 miles from Rovno.
And I told him in case I need something, he should introduce me that I'm his second or third cousin.
Now, after we escaped from the ghetto-- this was the third Aktion when they finished all the, the massacres-- I escaped that time under false papers because Dorothy was wounded in the leg.
And he helped me to establish contact with those of his cousins.
And the-- one of the girls who was secretary to the general supervising that town, she was his lover, or girlfriend, whatever.
So they gave me a job.
But they put me in a German uniform with a swastika on the arm.
And they gave me a rifle.
And I had to supervise the, these-- the Soviet war prisoners, how they're cutting down the trees in the forests.
Well, this was-- [LAUGHS] I told you it's like a time warp.
I didn't know where I-- where I am.
But Dorothy was in a hospital at that time because they cut off her finger, as she was wounded.
And she was, you know, on those false papers.
And I used to come into the hospital saying, heil, with a rifle and everything.
I tried to put it on very, very believable so they wouldn't suspect it because I was very insecure.
So I had to, to make it look or sound better than what actually it was.
So they shouldn't suspect anything.
Because if you hide and you walk around like this, you're not sure of yourself, somebody will pick it up.
So I had to act very secure and very pompous.
And that's what happened with my two friends that helped me tremendously.
They are-- one of them is in Florida-- the whole family a a matter of fact.
He didn't tell them that I'm Jewish until about a year after the war because he was afraid somebody might say something.
So they didn't know all the time while I was in that town, which called Slavuta, where Dorothy was in the hospital.
They didn't know all the time that I was there.
Did you look Jewish?
If I-- didn't-- no, I did not look Jewish.
You had blonde hair?
I had blonde hair, yes.
Especially when you put the uniform even on the dark hair, you look-- he looks uniformly, [INAUDIBLE].
Do you know what I mean?
So with a uniform, and rifle, and blond hair, I look like real.
But this wasn't the real army.
This was the engineering group called Todt organization.
Todt.
T-O-D-- T-O-D-T, Todt.
They are-- that's the engineer-- engineering group, group from the-- from the army.
But on Hitler's birthday or any other special thing, we had to go down the basement.
We had to drink beer.

And sitting at the same table with the SS men who just came in from, from looking at people, for the underground people from the woods, with all the German soldiers sitting at the same table drinking beer with them.
They had no idea I'm next to them.
I know.
I know.
That's very interesting.
The only thing-- twice it happened that I was in a panic because once everybody had to go for a medical exam.
And the second time I think was something similar.
And that's the time I came to the hospital.
I told Dorothy that something is-- I don't know what to do.
If I have to go for a medical exam or something, then I'm cooked.
Yeah.
So what happened is I slept in that-- what do you call-- Kaserne, in that place where all the other soldiers and SS-- everybody slept in that place.
It's a big [? camp. ?] So I got-- when she came out of the hospital, I took a room nearby.
I still worked for them.
But I took a room nearby.
And she was on crutches.
We lived in that room, not for too long.
I didn't.
Somehow-- I don't remember now how I escaped, and how I got out of it.
Because if I-- if I would have gotten for the medical exam, then forget it.
So that's what happened.
The same table.
Long table.
Yeah, all the SS men, and all the German army, and everybody.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Nope.
I don't think so.
There was one that I suspected, but I don't think he was.
Even if he was, I mean he didn't let me know.
And I didn't let him know.
I was afraid even to talk to anybody.
I spoke Polish and German.
N-- I had a Polish German acc-- German accent, but not Jewish accent, not a Jewish accent.
I was-- I was afraid.
I mean, it wasn't any-- you see, it's not easy, especially being-- hiding out like that.
But even hiding out after the second Aktion in Ostrog wasn't easy because you couldn't walk on the sidewalk.
You had to walk in the middle of the street.
When you saw a Ukrainian or German soldier you have to take off your hat.
You couldn't shake hands with a non-Jew.
And was-- I told you.
It's was like a time warp.
I mean, you didn't know where you were.
You don't know what will happen tomorrow.
You-- you're not optimistic.
You're not pessimistic.
You just work from minute to minute hoping for nothing.
You don't even know what to hope for.
Because we didn't know if the war was going good or bad.
We didn't have any newspapers or radio.
For a year and a half, we were completely isolated without any human contact, so to say, except my-- with Dorothy.
And nobody to talk to.
We are now in 1942 after we escaped from the ghetto.
Now, when we escaped--
Yeah, the third ghetto, the third--
--Massacre, third Aktion.
Now, when they surrounded that ghetto, I already had papers made out for me and Dorothy.
And I went to-- now, what I did, I established good communication with some policemen.
I drank with them, just in case.
As-- no.
This is Mark Grinims.
I mean, this was before, before the thing.
This was still while I was between my home and ghetto, back and forth, after the second Aktion.
So still Jews remained.
About 3,000 Jews remained in ghetto in the town.
But I established some type of-- just in case.
So I tried to be pally with them, you know, drink with them, talk to them.
They came to my house.
I gave him some soap.
I gave him this and that, so in case of something, to let me know what happens.
Now when we-- when they surrounded the ghetto, I saw one of the policemen that I just drank last night.
And I asked him to let me out to Dorothy.
And he says, no.
I'll let you out.
But I wouldn't let her out.
So I said, forget it.
So I went to another guy that I maybe drank two weeks ago.
And I gave him five gold pieces.
And I said, let me out.
So he did.
And that's how we escaped from the-- from the third Aktion together to the-- to certain, you know, household areas from people who lived around the area.
They noticed us, and that's why they squealed at night that we couldn't stay in that little room that her friends kept her.
We had to escape to another guy that I established good communication.
I slept for three or four months on the beets and potatoes in a subbasement and ate only once, probably [NON-ENGLISH] once a day--
--dumplings with milk.
And after a while, he couldn't-- no, I had to pay him too, naturally, with gold and diamonds and all that.
But after a while, he couldn't keep us because somebody squealed that we are talking.
And then that he remained with that woman, we went back.
And I went to some other man that I exchanged goods-- eggs, butter, and everything-- someplace 30-- about four or five miles from Ostrog.
He had a big, big farm.
And he also had a big-- he had a big farm.
And he had a son and a daughter.
He had some big plans when it comes to his daughter.
So when I came to hide out, he wanted to hide me.
But once he saw Dorothy, even though I told him it was my cousin, he didn't believe me.
And he says, I'm sorry.
I can't keep you both.
So I had to leave again.
He had some big plans then, you know?
And that's why we back-- back to the woman that Dorothy told you.
We were there.
And then-- but she couldn't keep us both.
She says, maybe for a while I'll keep you, but I can't keep two people even though I paid her too.
And then I went out to another place that I knew some girl from, from school yet, you know, I was dating her for a while or something.
I thought maybe they'll help me.
They helped me a little bit for a couple of days.
But again, they told me they can't hold me.
All three of them.
All three.
The first one, they only took the old people.
I told you, the sick.
The second one, they took all the young people, and I got away because I told them I'm a carpenter.
The third one in ghetto, we just plainly escaped because we had-- I, I, I paid gold the policemen, and we had the false papers made up.
Yeah, all three of them.
Yeah.
Had to go--
Well, no only two of them.
The third one was surrounded.
We escaped.
But only the two we had to go through.
That's the story.
Left anything out?
Let's see.
Then--
